title: Optimal Bet Sizing

content.statement: Bet a fraction of your capital equal to your edge divided by the odds.
content.explainer: This formula sizes bets to maximize long-run growth without risking ruin. Bet too big and one bad run wipes you out; bet too small and you leave compounding on the table, so the math ties your stake to how real your edge actually is.
questions: Do you honestly know your edge on this bet, or are you guessing at your win probability?
Are you sizing this position to survive a losing streak, or betting big because it feels certain?
How much of your total capital rides on any single bet right now?
sop: Define the specific bet and estimate your true probability of winning it, honestly and without optimism.
Calculate your edge as your expected advantage on the bet, then rate it as a decimal (see calculation, page 6).
Identify the payout odds you receive if you win (see calculation, page 6).
Divide edge by odds to get the fraction of capital to stake, and cap it to avoid betting on a shaky estimate.
Size every future bet by the same rule so no single loss can end your ability to keep playing.

definitions.def: Your statistical advantage on a bet: how much the odds are tilted in your favor. An edge of 0.2 means you expect to win 20 cents on the dollar over many repetitions.
The payout ratio if you win: odds of 2 means a winning bet returns two times the amount you staked. Higher odds mean a bigger payoff per unit risked.
The chance that a run of losses wipes out your capital entirely. Betting too large a fraction makes ruin almost certain over time, even with a real edge.

content.equation_example: With an Edge of 0.2 and Odds of 2: 0.2 ÷ 2 = 0.10, so bet 10% of your capital. A larger edge or shorter odds means bet more; a smaller edge or longer odds means bet less, and a zero or negative edge means do not bet at all.
content.closing_statement: Staying in the game long enough to compound beats any single bet you were sure could not lose.
content.question: Do you honestly know your edge on this bet, or are you guessing at your win probability?

caption:
Principle Nº 364: Optimal Bet Sizing

Bet a fraction of your capital equal to your edge divided by the odds.

Ask yourself: Do you honestly know your edge on this bet, or are you guessing at your win probability?
